Task
Materials:
4 dice per team
Recording sheet
Two-minute timer for each turn
Action:
Have students work in groups of 2 - 4.  Introduce the game with an example, and then have them play independently.  Discussion of "challenging rolls" afterwards can be productive.
Students roll the 4 dice to generate their seed numbers.  They then use those 4 numbers to create as many numbers as they can (1 - 10).  Scoring is done as in bowling; numbered "pins" are "knocked down" by creating an expression equal to the number.
The game can be structured in two different ways to assure that students are checking each other's expressions and verifying that they are written as intended:
During a student's turn, have them record just the expressions (not the intended result), and then pass the set to another student (a judge).  That judge then computes each expression as written and records which pins were knocked down.
Have the students play in teams.  Each team tries to achieve a "strike" (knocking down all of the pins, which is almost always possible).  Striving for the strike encourages students to brainstorm strategies for the "difficult" numbers, which leads them to discuss parts of each expression they have created already.

Write an expression that represents the number of ounces of flour left after they use what they need for the bread and cookies.
Then evaluate your expression to determine how much flour the baker has left.
b.   A different baker has 48 ounces of flour. They will use 6 ounces of flour for each cake that they make. Write an equation to show how many cakes they can make with all of their flour. Use

khan_exercises.description: Practice US customary length, volume, and mass problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit.

items.question_content:
Brooklyn purchased $3 \text { pounds}$ of pasta and $12 \text { ounces}$ of cheese to make macaroni and cheese.

**What is the total weight in ounces of the pasta and cheese combined?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\greenD{3}\text { pounds}$ to ounces.  

$1 \text{ pound}=\purpleD{16}\text{ ounces}$

So, $\greenD{3} \text{ pounds}=\greenD{3}\times \purpleD{16}=\blueD{48}\text{ ounces}$.

Brooklyn also purchased $\maroonD{12} \text{ ounces}$ of cheese.

We can add to find the total number of ounces for the pasta and cheese.

$\blueD{48} + \maroonD{12} = \goldE{60} \text { ounces}$
The total weight of the pasta and cheese combined is  $\goldE{60} \text { ounces}$.

Noa purchased $\dfrac34 \text { pound}$   of jelly beans and $3 \text { ounces}$ of sour gummies. 

**How many ounces of candy did Noa purchase all together?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

$1 \text{ pound}=\purpleD{16}\text{ ounces}$

So, $\greenD{\dfrac34} \text{ pound}=\greenD{\dfrac34}\times \purpleD{16}=\blueD{12}\text{ ounces}$.

Noa also purchased $\maroonD{3} \text{ ounces}$ of sour gummies.

We can add to find the total number of ounces of candy.

$\blueD{12} + \maroonD{3} = \goldE{15} \text { ounces}$
Noa purchased  $\goldE{15} \text { ounces}$ of candy all together.

items.question_content:
A bead shop sells their beads by the pound. A new shipment of beads weighing  $2 \text { pounds}$ is divided into $8$ equal-sized bags.   

**How many ounces of beads are in each bag?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\greenD{2}\text { pounds}$ to ounces.  

$1 \text{ pound}=\purpleD{16}\text{ ounces}$

So, $\greenD{2} \text{ pounds}=\greenD{2}\times \purpleD{16}=\blueD{32}\text{ ounces}$.

The beads are divided into $\maroonD8$ equal sized bags.

We can divide to find the number of ounces of beads in each bag.

$\blueD{32} \div \maroonD{8} = \goldE{4} \text { ounces}$
There are $\goldE4 \text { ounces}$ of beads in each bag.

items.question_content:
A teacher orders $20$ copies of a new graphic novel for his class to read. Each of the books weighs the same amount. The total weight of all the books is $10 \text { pounds}$.

**What is the weight, in ounces, of $1$ book?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\greenD{10}\text { pounds}$ to ounces.  

$1 \text{ pound}=\purpleD{16}\text{ ounces}$

So, $\greenD{10} \text{ pounds}=\greenD{10}\times \purpleD{16}=\blueD{160}\text{ ounces}$.

Each of the $\maroonD{20}$ books weighs the same amount.

We can divide to find how many ounces each book weighs.

$\blueD{160} \div \maroonD{20} = \goldE{8} \text { ounces}$
Each book weighs $\goldE8 \text { ounces}$.

items.question_content:
A box is filled with $20 \text{ pounds}$ of paper.  Brown paper takes up $\dfrac14$ of the box.

**How many ounces does the brown paper weigh?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\greenD{20}\text { pounds}$ to ounces.  

$1 \text{ pound}=\purpleD{16}\text{ ounces}$

So, $\greenD{20} \text{ pounds}=\greenD{20}\times \purpleD{16}=\blueD{320}\text{ ounces}$.

Brown paper takes up $\dfrac14$ of the box, so we can divide by $\maroonD4$ to find the weight of the brown paper.

$\blueD{320} \div \maroonD{4} = \goldE{80} \text { ounces}$
The brown paper weighs $\goldE{80} \text{ ounces}$.

items.question_content:
Tre is building a fence. He purchased $4 \text { pounds}$ of nails. He used $14 \text { ounces}$ of nails on his first day of building. 

**What is the weight, in ounces, of the remaining nails?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\greenD{4}\text { pounds}$ to ounces.  

$1 \text{ pound}=\purpleD{16}\text{ ounces}$

So, $\greenD{4} \text{ pounds}=\greenD{4}\times \purpleD{16}=\blueD{64}\text{ ounces}$.

Tre uses  $\maroonD{14} \text{ ounces}$ of nails the first day.

We can subtract to find the weight of the nails that are remaining.

$\blueD{64} - \maroonD{14} = \goldE{50} \text { ounces}$
There are $\goldE{50} \text { ounces}$ of nails remaining.

A sunflower grew $4 \text { inches}$ during the first week after it was planted. Then it grew an additional $4 \text { feet}$ over the next month.

**What is the total height of the sunflower in inches?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

First, we need to convert $\greenD{4}\text { feet}$ to inches.  

$1 \text{ foot}=\purpleD{12}\text{ inches}$

So, $\greenD{4} \text{ feet}=\greenD{4}\times \purpleD{12}=\blueD{48}\text{ inches}$.

The sunflower grew an additional $\maroonD{4} \text{ inches}$ during the first week.

We can add find out the height of the sunflower.

$\blueD{48} + \maroonD{4} = \goldE{52} \text { inches}$
The total height of the sunflower is $\goldE{52} \text { inches}$.

A woman takes $12$ equal-sized steps.  She walks a total of $10 \text { yards}$.

**How many inches long is each step?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\tealE{10}\text { yards}$ to inches.  

$1 \text{ yard}=\purpleE{36}\text{ inches}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

So, $\tealE{10} \text{ yards}=\tealE{10}\times \purpleE{36}=\blueE{360}\text{ inches}$.

We can divide to find the number of inches in each of the $\maroonE{12}$ steps.

$\blueE{360} \div \maroonE{12} = \goldE{30} \text{ inches}$
Each step is $\goldE{30} \text { inches}$ long.

A kangaroo takes one hop that is $4 \text { yards }2 \text { feet}$ long.

**How many feet did the kangaroo hop altogether?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$1 \text{ yard}=\purpleD{3}\text{ feet}$

So, $\greenD{4} \text{ yards}=\greenD{4}\times \purpleD{3}=\blueD{12}\text{ feet}$.

Next we need to add the additional $\maroonD{2} \text { feet}$.

$\blueD{12} + \maroonD{2} = \goldE{14} \text { feet}$
The kangaroo hopped $\goldE{14} \text { feet}$ all together.

items.question_content:
Jane buys $\dfrac73 \text { yard}$ of fabric to make a cat bed. She starts the project by cutting $4 \text { feet}$ of fabric off the end. 

**How many feet long is the remaining piece of fabric?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\greenD{\dfrac73}\text { yard}$ to feet.  

$1 \text{ yard}=\purpleD{3}\text{ feet}$

So, $\greenD{\dfrac73} \text{ yard}=\greenD{\dfrac73}\times \purpleD{3}=\blueD{7}\text{ feet}$.

She cuts off a piece of fabric that is $\maroonD{4} \text {  feet}$ long.

We can subtract to find the number of feet of fabric that remains.

$\blueD{7} - \maroonD{4} = \goldE{3} \text { feet}$
There are $\goldE{3} \text { feet}$ of fabric remaining.

So,

$\begin{align}
\blueE{1.8}\text{ L}&={\blueE{1.8}}\text{ L} \times {1{,}000}\text{ mL per L} \\\\
&= \blueE{1{,}800} \text{ mL}
\end{align}$

Now, we need to find out how much soup Gina had leftover.

Gina had a total of $\purpleE{9{,}350}\text{ mL}$ of soup. She and her kids ate $\blueE{1{,}800}\text{ mL}$ of soup.

Gina had $\maroonD{7{,}550}\text{ mL}$ of soup leftover.


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueE{1{,}800}\text { mL}~ +~ \maroonD{7{,}550}\text { mL} = {}\purpleE{9{,}350}\text{ mL}$
Gina had ${7{,}550} \text{ mL}$ of soup leftover.

[[☃ input-number 2]] $\text{L}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
George filled $10$ cups with $240\text{ mL}$ each.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{240}\text{ mL}\times\tealE{10}=~\purpleD{?}$

George used $\purpleD{2{,}400}\text{ mL}$ of coffee.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{240}\text{ mL}\times\tealE{10}=~\purpleD{2{,}400}\text{ mL}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{2{,}400}\text{ mL}$ to liters.

$1 \text{ mL} = \dfrac1{1{,}000}\text{ L}$

So,

$\begin {align}{\purpleD{2{,}400} \text{ mL}}&= {\purpleD{2{,}400}  \text{ 
mL}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{{1{,}000 \text{ mL}}} 
\\\\
&= {\dfrac{\purpleD{2{,}400}  \cancel{\text{ mL}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{{1{,}000 \cancel{\text{ mL}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{2{,}400} \text{ L} }{1{,}000}\\\\
&=\purpleD{2{,}400} \text{ L}\div1{,}000\\\\
&=\purpleD{2.4}\text{ L}
\end{align}$
George used $\purpleD{2.4} \text{ L}$ of coffee.

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{kg}$

hints:
Jim had $\blueE{19{,}700}\text { g}$ of sand and added  $\maroonD{6{,}300}\text { g}$ more.

$\blueE{19{,}700}\text { g} + \maroonD{6{,}300}\text { g} = {}\purpleE{?}$

Jim has $\purpleE{26{,}000}\text { g}$ of sand in his sandbox.

We need to convert $\purpleE{26{,}000}\text { g}$ to kilograms.  

$1 \text{ g}=\dfrac1{1{,}000}\text{ kg}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{26{,}000} \text{ kg}&=\purpleE{26{,}000}\text{ g}\times\dfrac{1\text{ kg}}{1{,}000\text{ g}}\\\\
&=\purpleE{26{,}000}\cancel{\text{ g}}\times\dfrac{1\text{ kg}}{1{,}000\cancel{\text{ g}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleE{26{,}000}\text{ kg}}{1{,}000}\\\\
&={\purpleE{26{,}000}\text{ kg}}\div{1{,}000}\\\\
&=\purpleE{26}\text{ kg}
\end{align}$
Jim has $\purpleE{26}\text{ kg}$ of sand in his sandbox.

Tessa is training for a marathon. She runs $13\text{ km}$ each day for $3$ days.

**How  many *meters* did Tessa run in total?**  


[[☃ input-number 2]] meters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Tessa ran $13\text{ km}$ each day for $3$ days.



[[☃ image 1]]

$ \tealE{3} \times{}\maroonD{13}\text{ km} = ~\purpleD{?}$

Tessa ran a total of $\purpleD{39} \text{ km}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$ \tealE{3} \times{}\maroonD{13}\text{ km} = ~\purpleD{39}\text{ km}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{39}\text{ km}$ to meters.

$1 \text{ km} = 1{,}000\text{ m}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{39}\text{ km}&=\purpleD{39}\text{ km} \times {1{,}000}\text{ m per km}\\\\
 &= \purpleD{39{,}000} \text{ m}
\end{align}$
Tessa ran a total of $\purpleD{39{,}000} \text{ m}$.

khan_exercises.title: Multi-step word problems with whole numbers
khan_exercises.description: Solve multi-step word problems using any of the four basic operations. Interpret remainders and their effect on the quotient.
topic_path: Multi-step word problems with whole numbers

items.question_content:
The art club raised $\$248$ through fundraisers.  Then they spent $\$206$ to set up an art show.  They raised another  $\$316$ at the art show.

**How much money did the art club have after the show?** 

$\$$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The club started with $\$248$ and then spent $\$206$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{248}-\blueE{206}=\maroonD{42}$

The club had $\$\maroonD{42}$ left after they spent for the art show.

Then, they raised another $\$316$ at the show.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{42}+\goldE{316}=\greenE{358}$
The club had $\$\greenE{358}$ after the show.

items.question_content:
After collecting eggs from his chickens, Dale puts the eggs into cartons to sell. Dale fills $15$ cartons and has $7$ eggs left over. Each carton holds $12$ eggs.

**How many eggs did Dale collect?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] eggs
hints:
We can multiply to find the total number of eggs in all $15$ cartons.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{12} \times 15 = \purpleD{180}$

We can add to find the total number of eggs, including the $\greenE{7}$ that were left over.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{180} +\greenE{7} = \redE{187}$
Dale collected $\redE{187}$ eggs.

items.question_content:
A class sold tickets to their school play. Each of the $23$ students in the class sold $4$ tickets. The cost of each ticket was $\$7$. 

**How much money did the class earn by selling tickets to the play?**

$\$$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: The class raised $\$\goldE{644}$ by selling tickets to the play.

items.question_content:
Abit is planning a birthday party for his granddad. He bought a cake for $\$18$. He also wants to buy some balloons, which are $\$4$ each. Abit has $\$35$ to spend all together.

**How much money will Abit have left after he buys the cake and as many balloons as possible?**

 $\$$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
We can subtract to find out how much money Abit will have left after he buys the cake.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{35}-\greenE{18} = \purpleD{17}$

Abit has up to $\purpleD{17}$ dollars left for balloons.

Each balloon costs $\$4$. We can divide the amount of money Abit has left after buying the cake by $4$ to find out how many balloons he can buy.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{17}\div\maroonD{4} = \goldE{4}\text{ R } \blueE{1}$

Abit can buy $\goldE{4}$ balloons and will have $\blueE{1}$ dollar left.
Abit will have $\$ \blueE{1}$ left.

items.question_content:
A PE class has $48$ students. One group of $24$ students chooses to play volleyball. The teacher then divides the remaining students into as many groups of $5$ as possible to shoot baskets.   After that, the remaining students (not on a volleyball or basketball team) climb the rock wall.

**How many students climb the rock wall?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] students
hints:
We can subtract to find the total number of students who do not want to play volleyball.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{48}-\greenE{24}=\purpleD{24}$

There are $\purpleD{24}$ students who do not want to play volleyball.

We can divide to put those students into groups of $5$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{24}\div\maroonD{5}= \goldE{4} \text{ R } \blueE{4}$

There will be $\goldE{4}$ groups with $\maroonD{5}$ students and $\blueE{4}$ students still remaining to climb the rock wall.
$\blueE{4}$ students will climb the rock wall.

items.question_content:
Sanji scored $125$ points in the first round of a video game and $263$ points in the second round. His total score after the third round was $557$ points.

**How many more points did Sanji score in the third round of the video game than in the first round?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] points
hints:
We can add to find Sanji's total points for the first two rounds.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{125}+\goldE{263}=\greenE{388}$

Sanji had $388$ points from the first two rounds.

Then, we can subtract to find out how many points Sanji scored in the third round.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{557}-\greenE{388}=\purpleD{169}$

Sanji scored $\purpleD{169}$ points in the third round.

We can subtract to find how many more points Sanji scored in the third round than in the first round.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{169}-\maroonD{125}=\tealE{44}$
Sanji scored $\tealE{44}$ more points in the third round than in the first round.

items.question_content:
Erin had $55$ pebbles that she collected from hikes. She took out her favorite $7$ pebbles and then equally divided the other pebbles among her $3$ sisters. Erin's youngest sister, Su, already had $15$ pebbles.

**How many pebbles does Su have now?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pebbles
hints:
We can subtract to find the number of pebbles Erin gave away.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{55}-\maroonD{7} = \tealE{48}$

Erin gave away $\tealE{48}$ pebbles.

She divided the pebbles she gave away equally among her $3$ sisters.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\tealE{48} \div 3 = \goldE{16}$

Erin gave each sister, including Su, $\goldE{16}$ pebbles.

Su already had $\blueE{15}$ pebbles. We can add to find her total.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{16} + \blueE{15} = \redE{31}$
Su has $\redE{31}$ pebbles now.

items.question_content:
An ice cream shop is testing some new flavors of ice cream. They invent $25$ new flavors for customers to try, and throw out the $13$ least popular flavors.  The shop makes $80$ cartons of each of the remaining flavors. It takes $2$ cartons to get one liter $(\text{L})$ of ice cream.

**How much total ice cream is in the cartons?**
 
[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{L}$
hints:
We can subtract to find out how many new flavors of ice cream the shop made.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{25}-\goldE{13}=\blueE{12}$

The shop had $\blueE{12}$ remaining new flavors.
There were $\redE{480}\,\text{L}$ of ice cream in the cartons.

items.question_content:
A farmer had $88$ melons growing in her garden. In June, the farmer had picked $14$ melons. In July, a thunderstorm knocked a branch from a tree and crushed half the melons that remained in the garden. Then the farmer picked the rest.

**How many melons did the farmer pick in June and July combined?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] melons
hints:
We can subtract to find the number of melons that were left in the garden in June.



[[☃ image 1]]

 $\purpleD{88}-\goldE{14}=\blueE{74}$

There were $\blueE{74}$ melons left in the garden at the end of June.

The branch crushed half the melons that remained in the garden, and the farmer picked the remaining half. So we can divide to find out how many melons the branch crushed and how many were picked.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{74} \div 2= \maroonD{37}$

The branch crushed $\maroonD{37}$ melons, so the farmer picked the remaining $\maroonD{37}$ melons.

To find the total amount of melons the farmer picked in June and July, we can add the total amount of melons picked in each month.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{14}+\maroonD{37} = \greenE{51}$
The farmer picked $\greenE{51}$ melons in all.

items.question_content:
Peter scored $15$ goals during his second soccer season as a forward. He scored $6$ fewer goals during his first season. The team as a whole scored $4$ times as many goals as Peter during the two seasons.

**How many goals did the team score during the two seasons?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] goals
hints:
Peter scored $6$ fewer goals in the first season than in the second. We can subtract to find Peter's number of goals in the first season.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{15}-\maroonD{6}=\greenE{9}$

Peter scored $\greenE{9}$ goals in the first season.

We can add to find how many total goals Peter scored.



[[☃ image 1]]

 $\greenE{9}+\purpleD{15}= \blueE{24}$

Peter scored $\blueE{24}$ goals during both seasons.

The team as a whole scored $4$ times as many goals as Peter. We can multiply to find the number of goals the whole team scored.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{24} \times 4 = \redE{96}$
The team scored $\redE{96}$ goals during the two seasons.

items.question_content:
Abigail buys two cartons of strawberries. One carton has $19$ berries and the other carton has $26$ berries. She wants to divide the berries into bags so there are exactly $6$ berries in each bag.

**How many bags will have $6$ berries?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] bags
hints:
We can add to find the total number of berries in both cartons.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{19}+\maroonD{26} = \purpleD{45}$

There were $\purpleD{45}$ total berries

Abigail wants to divide the berries into groups of $6$. We can divide by $6$ to see how many bags she will need for all the berries.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{45}\div \greenE{6} = \redE{7} \text{ R } \goldE{3}$

$\redE{7}$ bags will have $\greenE{6}$ berries and $1$ bag will have $\goldE{3}$ berries.

We only need the number of full bags of $6$ berries.
$\redE{7}$ bags will have $6$ berries.

items.question_content:
A group of $4$ friends is playing cards. The deck has $70$ cards. To start the game, the dealer makes a pile of $15$ cards  in the center. Then she deals the remaining cards one at a time to each player until all the cards are gone.  

**What is the greatest number of cards any player will have after all the cards are dealt?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] cards
hints:
We can subtract to find out how many cards will be dealt to the players.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{70}-\blueE{15}=\maroonD{55}$

There are $\maroonD{55}$ cards to deal.

Next, we can divide the number of cards to deal by the number of players.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{55}\div \greenD{4} = \tealE{13} \text{ R } \goldE{3}$

The $\goldE{3}$ extra cards will also go to players.
The *least* number of cards per player is $\tealE{13}$ cards, but $\goldE{3}$ players get an extra card. So $\goldE{3}$ players get $\redE{14}$ cards each.
The *greatest* number of cards any player will have after all the cards are dealt is $\redE{14}$.

items.question_content:
Jordan's mom bought $7$ packs of juice boxes for the team to drink after practice. Each pack had $8$ boxes of juice. After the team drank as much juice as they wanted, there were $29$ boxes of juice remaining. The team only opened each pack after they drank all the boxes from the pack before.

**How many packs of juice  did the team open?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] packs
hints:
We can multiply to find the number of boxes of juice Jordan's mom bought in $7$ packs.



[[☃ image 1]]

$7\times \blueE{8}=\redE{56}$

Jordan's mom bought $\redE{56}$ juice boxes.

If we subtract the $29$ boxes of juice that were left from the total, we will know how many boxes of juice the team drank.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{56}-\greenE{29} = \purpleD{27}$

The team drank $\purpleD{27}$ boxes of juice.

We can divide the boxes they drank by $8$ to see how many packs the team opened.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{27} \div \blueE{8} = \goldE{3} \text{ R }\maroonD{3}$

The team drank $\goldE{3}$ full packs and $1$ partial pack.
The team opened $4$ packs of juice.

items.question_content:
A squirrel family collected $172$ nuts to store for the winter. They spread the nuts out evenly among their $6$ favorite spots. The squirrels ate the last few nuts that didn't divide evenly. Sadly, a crow stole half the nuts from one of the spots.

**How many nuts did the crow steal?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] nuts
hints:
We can divide to find out how many nuts were in each of the $6$ spots.



[[☃ image 1]]

 $\purpleD{172}\div 6=\maroonD{28}\text{ R }\blueE{4}$

The family ate the nuts left over, so there were $\maroonD{28}$ nuts per spot.

The crow stole half the nuts from one spot.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{28} \div 2 = \greenE{14}$
The crow stole $\greenE{14}$ nuts.

items.question_content:
The drama group has $90$ minutes to practice two scenes. They want to practice the pirate scene $4$ times, and each time takes $13$ minutes. The mermaid scene takes $7$ minutes. 

**How many full times can the group practice the mermaid scene?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] times
hints:
We can multiply to find how long it takes to practice the pirate scene $4$ times.



[[☃ image 1]]

$4\times \maroonD{13}=\blueE{52}$

The group will practice the pirate scene for $\blueE{52}$ minutes.

If we subtract the $\blueE{52}$ minutes from the total, we will know how much time is left.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{90}-\blueE{52} = \purpleD{38}$

There will be $\purpleD{38}$ minutes left to practice the mermaid scene.

We can divide to see how many times the group can practice the mermaid scene.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{38} \div \greenE{7} = \tealE{5} \text{ R }\goldE{3}$

The group has time for $\tealE{5}$ full times with $\goldE{3}$ minutes left over.

That is not enough time to practice the scene one more time.
The group can practice the mermaid scene $\tealE{5}$ full times.

khan_exercises.description: Solve word problems by comparing decimals
topic_path: Module 1: Place value and decimal fractions

items.question_content:
Owen  and Jessica use a stopwatch to time how long it takes them to run through an obstacle course at a playground. Owen's time was $38.19$ seconds. Jessica's time was $38.9$ seconds.

**Who completed the obstacle course in less time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We need to figure out who's time is shorter.

Let's get both times to the same place value, the hundredths.

$\blueD{38.19~}=\blueD{38.19}=\blueD{3819} \text { hundredths}$

$\greenD{38.9~~}=\greenD{38.90}=\greenD{3890} \text { hundredths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{3819}<\greenD{3890}$
**Owen** completed the obstacle course in less time.

items.question_content:
Sam is keeping track of the distances at a long jump competition. The jumper from Eastside High jumped $7.85$ meters. The jumper from Temple High jumped $7.1$ meters.

**Which school had a longer jump?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We need to figure out which jump is longer.

Let's get both distances to the same place value, the hundredths.

$\blueD{7.85}=\blueD{7.85}=\blueD{785} \text { hundredths}$

$\greenD{7.1~~}=\greenD{7.10}=\greenD{710} \text { hundredths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{785}>\greenD{710}$
The jumper from **Eastside High** had a longer jump.

items.question_content:
An archaeologist measures the length of two dinosaur tooth fossils. He wants to display the tooth that is larger at a museum. Tooth A is $0.23$ meters long. Tooth B is $0.195$ meters long.

**Which tooth should be displayed at the museum?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We need to figure out which tooth is longer.

Let's get both lengths to the same place value, the thousandths.

$\blueD{0.23~~}=\blueD{0.230}=\blueD{230} \text { thousandths}$

$\greenD{0.195}=\greenD{0.195}=\greenD{195} \text { thousandths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{230}>\greenD{195}$
**Tooth A** should be displayed at the museum.

items.question_content:
Marie is choosing a bag of apples to make into a pie. She thinks the pie will be tastier if it has more apples. The bag of green apples weighs $4.75$ pounds. The bag of red apples weighs $4.5$ pounds. 

**Which bag of apples should she buy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We need to figure out which bag of apples is heavier.

Let's get both weights to the same place value, the hundredths.

$\blueD{4.75}=\blueD{4.75}=\blueD{475} \text { hundredths}$

$\greenD{4.5~~}=\greenD{4.50}=\greenD{450} \text { hundredths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{475}>\greenD{450}$
Marie should choose the **green apples**.

items.question_content:
Al is comparing two recipes for home made bread. The recipe for white bread calls for $0.4$ kg of flour. The recipe for whole wheat bread calls for $0.385$ kg of flour.

**Which bread takes less flour?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We need to figure out which type of bread needs less flour.


Let's get both weights to the same place value, the thousandths.

$\blueD{0.4~~~}=\blueD{0.400}=\blueD{400} \text { thousandths}$

$\greenD{0.385}=\greenD{0.385}=\greenD{385} \text { thousandths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\greenD{385}<\blueD{400}$
The **whole wheat bread** takes less flour.

items.question_content:
Kesia is writing a report about two of  the world's fastest fish. She wants to put the faster fish on the cover of her report. The fastest speed for a sailfish is $67.85$ mph. The fastest speed for a swordfish is $60$ mph.

**Which fish should go on the cover of Kesia's report?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's get both speeds to the same place value, the hundredths.

$\blueD{67.85}=\blueD{67.85}=\blueD{6785} \text { hundredths}$

$\greenD{60~~~~}=\greenD{60.00}=\greenD{6000} \text { hundredths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{6785}>\greenD{6000}$
The **Sailfish** should go on the cover of Kesia's report.

items.question_content:
Gus is measuring earth worms that he digs up in his backyard. The worm he found in the garden was $8.9$ cm long. The worm he found under the porch was $9$ cm long.

**Which worm is longer?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's get both lengths to the same place value, the tenths.

$\blueD{8.9}=\blueD{8.9}=\blueD{89} \text { tenths}$

$\greenD{9~~}=\greenD{9.0}=\greenD{90} \text { tenths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\green{90}>\blue{89}$
**The worm from underneath the porch** is longer.

choices.content: The worm from underneath the porch

items.question_content:
At Parker Elementary, students travel an average of $3.375$ miles to get to school. At Franklin Elementary, students travel an average of $3.35$ miles.

**Which school has the higher average?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's get both distances to the same place value, the thousandths.

$\blueD{3.375~}=\blueD{3.375}=\blueD{3375} \text { thousandths}$

$\greenD{3.35~~}=\greenD{3.350}=\greenD{3350} \text { thousandths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{3375}>\greenD{3350}$
**Parker Elementary** has the higher average.

items.question_content:
Abril wants a cold drink to take with her to the park. She is choosing between a bottle of sparkling water that contains  $502.75 \text{ mL}$   and a bottle of plain water that contains $499.793 \text{ mL}$.  It is a hot day and Abril wants to bring as much to drink as possible.

**Which beverage should Abril choose?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's get both volumes to the same place value, the thousandths.

$\blueD{502.75~~}=\blueD{502.750}=\blueD{502{,}750} \text { thousandths}$

$\greenD{499.793}=\greenD{499.793}=\greenD{499{,}793} \text { thousandths}$

Now that numbers are in the same place value, we can order them.

$\blueD{502{,}750}>\greenD{499{,}793}$
Abril should choose the **sparkling water**.

items.question_content:
The local basketball championship game will be held between the Davis Tigers and the Skyview Ravens. The Tigers have a player that is $76.75$ inches tall. The Ravens have a player that is $76.125$ inches tall.

**Which team has the taller player?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

khan_exercises.title: Multiplication and division word problems
khan_exercises.description: Practice solving multiplication and division word problems. Some problems have remainders.

items.question_content:
Skylar has $4$ times as many books as Karen.

**If Skylar has $164$ books, how many books does Karen have?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's use $K$ for the number of Karen's books. We can make a model to show the given information.

[[☃ image 1]]

What is the value of $K$ if $4$ groups of $K$ is the same as $164$ books?

We can write this as a multiplication problem.

$4\times K=164$

We can rewrite the relationship with division.

$164\div 4 = K$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{4}&\overline{\Big)164}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{160}}&\purpleD{40}&\maroonD{4} \times \purpleD{40} = \blueE{160}\\
&4\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{4}&\overline{\Big)164}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&40\\
&4\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{4}}&\purpleD{1}&\maroonD{4} \times \purpleD{1} = \blueE{4}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{4}&\overline{\Big)164}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&\purpleD{40}\\
&4\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{4}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{1}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{41}\\
\end{array}$

[[☃ image 1]]

Karen has $41$ books, because $164 \div 4=41$.

items.question_content:
Tannis's first machine could throw a car $27$ meters. She studied her first machine and built a better one. Her second machine could throw a car $7$ times as far.

**How far does the second machine throw a car?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's use $d$ for the distance the second machine can throw the car. We can make a model to show the given information.

[[☃ image 1]]

How far is $d$ if $d$ is $7$ times as far as $27$ meters?

We can write this as a multiplication problem.

$27\times 7=d$

$\begin{align}
27&\\
\underline{{} \times 7}&\\
\purpleD{49}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{140}}\\
189
\end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The machine throws the car $189$ meters, because $27 \times 7 =189$.

choices.content: The machine throws the car $189$ meters, because $27 \times 7 =189$.
The machine throws the car $3$ meters, because $27 \div 7 =3$ remainder $6$.
The machine throws the car $4$ meters, because $27 \div 7 =3$ remainder $6$.

items.question_content:
Jenna's dogs used $9$ chew toys last year. The animal shelter used $2{,}277$ chew toys. 

**How many times as many chew toys did the animal shelter use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: $\text{chew toys animal shelter used}={?} \times \text{chew toys Jenna's dogs used}$

We can fill in the given information.

$2{,}277 = {?} \times 9$

We can rewrite the relationship with division.

$2{,}277 \div 9 = {?}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)2{,}277}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}800}}&\purpleD{200}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{200} = \blueE{1{,}800}\\
&477\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)2{,}277}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&200\\
&477\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{450}}&\purpleD{50}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{50} = \blueE{450}\\
&27\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)2{,}277}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&200\\
&477\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{450}&50\\
&27\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{27}}&\purpleD{3}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{3} = \blueE{27}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{9}&\overline{\Big)2{,}277}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&\purpleD{200}\\
&477\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{450}&\purpleD{50}\\
&27\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{27}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{3}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{253}\\
\end{array}$
The animal shelter used $253$ times as many chew toys, because $2{,}277 \div 9 = 253$.

choices.content: The animal shelter used $20{,}493$ times as many chew toys, because $9 \times 2{,}277 = 20{,}493$.
The animal shelter used $2{,}053$ times as many chew toys, because $2{,}277 \div 9 = 2{,}053$.
The animal shelter used $253$ times as many chew toys, because $2{,}277 \div 9 = 253$.

items.question_content:
A seal has $1{,}500$ nerves per whisker. That is $10$ times as many as a rat has.

**How many nerves per whisker does a rat have?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's use $r$ for the number of nerves per rat whisker. We can make a model to show the given information.

[[☃ image 1]]

What is the value of $r$ if $10$ times as many as $r$ is equal to $1{,}500$?

We can write this as a multiplication problem.

$10\times r=1{,}500$

We can rewrite the relationship with division.

$1{,}500\div 10 = r$

When **dividing** by $10$, we shift our numbers $1$ place value to the right.

Number | Thousands | Hundreds | Tens | Ones
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1{,}500$ |$\searrow\goldD{1}$ | $\searrow\purpleD{5}$ | $\searrow\blueD{0}$ | $\searrow\greenD{0}$  
$1{,}500 \div 10$ |$?$ |$?$ |$?$| $?$

Number | Thousands | Hundreds | Tens | Ones
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1{,}500$ |$\searrow\goldD{1}$ | $\searrow\purpleD{5}$ | $\searrow\blueD{0}$ | $\searrow\greenD{\cancel0}$  
$1{,}500 \div 10$ | |$\goldD{1}$ | $\purpleD{5}$ |$\blueD{0}$

[[☃ image 1]]

A rat has $150$ nerves per whisker because $1{,}500 \div 10 = 150$.

choices.content: A rat has $15{,}000$ nerves per whisker because $10 \times 1{,}500 = 15{,}000$.
A rat has $150$ nerves per whisker because $1{,}500 \div 10 = 150$.
A rat has $1{,}510$ nerves per whisker because $1{,}500 \times 10 = 1{,}510$.

items.question_content:
Ares is making $14$ jars of honey peanut butter. He wants to use $45$ milliliters $(\text{mL})$ of honey in each jar.

We can write this as a multiplication problem.

$14\times 45=h$

$\begin{align}
1\blueE{4}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{4}5}&\\
20\\
50\\
\greenE{160}&\quad \maroonD{40} \times \blueE{4} = \greenE{160}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{1}4&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{4}5}&\\
20\\
50\\
160\\
\underline{{}+\greenE{400}}&\quad \maroonD{40} \times \blueE{10} = \greenE{400}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
14&\\
\underline{{} \times 45}&\\
\purpleD{20}\\
\purpleD{50}\\
\purpleD{160}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{400}}\\
630
\end{align}$

Ares uses $630\,\text{mL}$ of honey, because $14\times 45=630$.

choices.content: Ares uses $1\,\text{mL}$ of honey, because $14 \div 45=0$, remainder $14$.

What is the value of $p$ if $4$ groups of $p$ is the same as $131$?

We can rewrite the relationship with division.

$\begin{array}{rrrl}
&p\\
{4}&\overline{\Big)131}\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{4}&\overline{\Big)131}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{120}}&\purpleD{30}&\maroonD{4} \times \purpleD{30} = \blueE{120}\\

&11\\

items.question_content:
Louis made $24 \text{ quarts}$ of lemonade. He divided the lemonade equally between $6$ pitchers.  

**How  many *quarts* of  lemonade did Louis put in each pitcher?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] quarts

Convert the number of quarts to pints.

**How  many *pints* of  lemonade did Louis put in each pitcher?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] pints

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Louis divided $24\text{ quarts}$ into $6$ pitchers.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{24}\text{ quarts } \div \goldE6 ={} \maroonD{?}\text{ quarts}$

Each pitcher contained $\maroonD4 \text{ quarts}$ of lemonade.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{24}\text{ quarts } \div \goldE6 = \maroonD{4}\text{ quarts}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD4\text{ quarts}$ to pints.

$1 \text{ quart} = 2\text{ pints}$

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD4\text{ quarts}&=\maroonD4\text{ quarts} \times {2\text{ pints per quart}} \\\\
&= \maroonD8 \text{ pints}
\end{align}$

Louis put $\maroonD4$ quarts of lemonade in each pitcher.

Louis put $\maroonD8$ pints of lemonade in each pitcher.

hints:
Bob started with $22$ pints of soda. At the end of the party, there were $8$ pints left.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\purpleD{?} + \blueE{8}\text{ pints} = \greenD{22}\text{ pints}$

$14$ pints of soda were drank during the party.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{14}\text{ pints} + \blueE{8}\text{ pints} = \maroonD{22}\text{ pints}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{14}\text{ pints}$ to cups.

So,

A fish tank has $240$ quarts of water. Levi adds another $120$ quarts to the tank.

**How  many *quarts* of  water are in the fish tank?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] quarts

Convert the number of quarts to gallons.

**How  many *gallons* of  water are in the fish tank?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] gallons

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
The fish tank has $240$ quarts of water. Levi adds $120$ quarts.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{240}\text{ quarts}  + \blueE{120}\text{ quarts} = \maroonD{?}\text{ quarts}$

There is a total capacity of $360$ quarts.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{240}\text{ quarts}  + \blueE{120}\text{ quarts}= \maroonD{360}\text{ quarts}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD{360}\text{ quarts}$ to gallons.

$1 \text{ quart} =\dfrac{1}{4}\text{ gallon}$

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{360}\text{ quarts}&=\maroonD{360}\text{ quarts} \times \dfrac{1\text{ gallon}}{4\text{ quarts}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{360}\cancel{\text{ quarts}}}{1} \times \dfrac{1\text{ gallon}}{4\cancel{\text{ quarts}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{360}\text{ gallons}}{4}\\\\
&={\maroonD{360}\text{ gallons}}\div{4}\\\\
&= \maroonD{90} \text{ gallons}
\end{align}$

There are $\maroonD{360}$ quarts of water in the fish tank.

There are $\maroonD{90}$ gallons of water in the fish tank.

&= \goldE{128} \text{ ounces}
\end{align}$

Now, we can add the $1$ ounce.

$\goldE{128}\text{ ounces} + 1\text{ ounce} = \goldE{129}\text{ ounces}$

Now, we can find the difference between Katie and Xan's weight.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{129}\text{ oz} - \purpleD{105}\text{ oz} = ~\blueE{?}$

The difference is $\blueE{24}\text{ ounces}$.

Xan's weight is $\purpleD{105}\text{ ounces}$.

items.question_content:
A kangaroo hopped first $3{,}520$ yards to the lake with her baby in her pouch.  She hopped the remaining $5{,}280$ yards without her baby in her pouch. 

**How many *yards* did the kangaroo hop to the lake?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] yards

Convert the number of yards to miles.

**How many *miles* did the kangaroo hop to the lake?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] miles

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
The kangaroo hopped $3{,}520$ yards with her baby in her pouch. She hopped $5{,}280$ yards without her baby in her pouch.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{3{,}520}\text{ yds} + \maroonD{5{,}280}\text{ yds} = ~\purpleD{?}$

The kangaroo hopped $8{,}800$ yards to the lake.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{3{,}520}\text{ yds} + \maroonD{5{,}280}\text{ yds} = ~\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yds}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yds}$ to miles.

$1 \text { yard} = \dfrac{1}{1{,}760}\text{ mile}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yards}&=\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yards} \times \dfrac{1\text{ miles}}{{1{,}760}\text{ yards}}\\\\
&=\purpleD{8{,}800}\cancel{\text{ yards}} \times \dfrac{1\text{ miles}}{{1{,}760}\cancel{\text{ yards}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ miles}}{1{,}760}\\\\
&=\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ miles}\div{1{,}760}\\\\
&=\purpleD5\text{ miles}
\end{align}$

The kangaroo hopped $\purpleD{8{,}800}$ yards.

The kangaroo hopped $\purpleD{5}$ miles.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{2{,}000}\text{ feet}\times\blueE6 = \purpleD{?}$

Shawntell ran $12{,}000$ feet altogether.

$\maroonD{2{,}000}\text{ feet}\times\blueE6 = \purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}$ to yards.

$1 \text { foot} = \dfrac{1}{3}\text{ yard}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}&=\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}\times \dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{{3}\text{ feet}}\\\\
&=\purpleD{12{,}000}\cancel{\text{ feet}} \times \dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{{3}\cancel{\text{ feet}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ yards}}{3}\\\\
&=\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ yards}\div{3}\\\\
&=\purpleD{4{,}000}\text{ yards}
\end{align}$

Shawntell ran $\purpleD{12{,}000}$ feet.

Shawntell ran $\purpleD{4{,}000}$ yards.
